issuetype.description: An improvement or enhancement to an existing feature or task.
issuetype.name: Improvement

fields.description: It's very common for user code to create things in /tmp. Yes, applications have means to specify alternate tmp directories but doing so is opt-in and therefore doesn't happen in many case.  At a minimum, linux can use private namespaces to create a private /tmp for each container so that it's using the same space allocated to containers and it's automatically cleaned up as part of container clean-up.

comments.body: Patch that uses linux private namespace and bind mounts to achieve a private /tmp.

This patch doesn't address localization. Thinking was that localization doesn't run application code so while it may create files in /tmp (e.g. hsperf*), I wouldn't expect that to be a significant problem. I can look into addressing localization as well if folks think it's important.

This message was automatically generated.

Thanks for the patch, [~nroberts]!

I think minimally this needs to be an optional feature (either at the cluster level or at the per-job level) that defaults to false.  There may be cluster setups where tasks are interacting with services outside of YARN and communicate with those services via /tmp.  (Yes, they should be using /var/run or something like that, but maybe they're not conforming to typical paths for that.)  Otherwise I worry we could break an existing use-case with no possibility to work around it other than downgrading to a version without this feature.

bq. I think minimally this needs to be an optional feature (either at the cluster level or at the per-job level) that defaults to false.
I agree. I'm thinking that a cluster-level config with default=off, and then the best way to handle container-level support would be via the ongoing work to support docker containers.
